story.headline: US and Iran trade strikes for a second straight day; Trump says the ceasefire is 'over'
story.summary: US CENTCOM struck ~90 Iranian coastal military targets; Iran retaliated against US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain and Qatar over Strait of Hormuz navigation.
story.display_body: US Central Command (CENTCOM) said it struck roughly 90 Iranian military targets late Wednesday — missile and drone storage and logistics sites along Iran's coast — framing the raids as aimed at Iran's 'ability to threaten the freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz.' Iran's Revolutionary Guard and army retaliated Thursday against US bases in the Gulf, saying they hit a Patriot air-defence battery in Kuwait, a satellite antenna in Qatar, and fuel depots in Bahrain; Kuwait reported intercepting missiles and drones and Qatar issued an 'elevated security threat' alert. US strikes also hit a railway bridge in Iran's northeast and a base in coastal Bushehr, suspending the Tehran-Mashhad train line. Trump, flying back from the NATO summit in Turkiye, said Tehran had called wanting 'to make a deal so badly.'
story.why: This is the widest US-Iran exchange since the spring war and directly threatens the strait that carried about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and gas before the crisis.

story.headline: IMF cuts 2026 global growth forecast to 3%, citing Iran war energy shock
story.summary: IMF trimmed world growth to 3% and lifted 2026 inflation to 4.7%, partly offset by an AI investment boom; Brent surged past $79.
story.display_body: In its new World Economic Outlook (released Wednesday), the International Monetary Fund trimmed world growth to 3% (from 3.1% in April) — its second downgrade this year — and raised its 2026 inflation forecast to 4.7%. The Fund cast the outlook as 'two powerful forces pulling in opposite directions': the energy shock from the Middle East war versus an AI-driven investment boom that partly offsets it. The forecast assumes Hormuz starts reopening in mid-July; shipping there ran at 41 verified transits Tuesday versus ~130 daily before the war, and Brent crude jumped as much as 7% past $79 a barrel after strikes resumed.
story.why: The number puts a figure on the war's global cost and signals higher-for-longer inflation and energy prices for European importers, Switzerland included.

story.headline: Trump begins removing Syria from the US state-sponsor-of-terrorism list
story.summary: State Department opened a 45-day rescission of Syria's 1979 terrorism designation after assurances from President al-Sharaa.
story.display_body: The State Department said President Trump notified Congress of his intent to rescind Syria's designation, opening a 45-day pre-notification period; Syria has held the label since 1979 — the longest of any country. The move follows a June 2025 executive order easing sanctions and 'formal assurances' from Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa (a former al-Qaeda-affiliate commander who broke with the group in 2016) that Damascus will not support international terrorism.
story.why: Delisting would unlock trade, banking and investment for a country trying to rebuild after its civil war and marks a sharp US pivot toward the new Syrian government.

story.headline: US to license Ukraine to build its own Patriot interceptors
story.summary: At the Ankara NATO summit Trump granted Kyiv a licence to co-produce Patriot air-defence missiles, though output could take years.
story.display_body: At the Ankara NATO summit, Trump said Washington will grant Kyiv a production licence to co-manufacture Patriot air-defence missiles — the interceptors central to stopping Russian ballistic strikes — after months of Ukrainian lobbying. He cautioned that the prime contractors (Lockheed Martin and RTX) had not yet been informed and that standing up production could take years.
story.why: Domestic Patriot output would ease Ukraine's chronic shortage of interceptors, but the years-long timeline means little near-term relief this fighting season.

story.headline: DR Congo Ebola outbreak passes 600 deaths as health workers strike over unpaid wages
story.summary: DRC confirmed 600 deaths and 1,759 Ebola cases; Ituri front-line workers walk off jobs over wages unpaid since the May 15 outbreak declaration.
story.display_body: Government data put confirmed deaths at 600 and cases at 1,759 (51 new cases and 20 deaths in 24 hours), with two suspected cases now in Kisangani, one of the country's largest cities. Front-line workers in hardest-hit Ituri province have begun striking, telling AP they have not been paid since the outbreak was declared on 15 May and are working with limited protective gear.
story.why: A walkout by the responders now would cripple containment just as the virus reaches a major urban centre.

story.headline: Tropical Storm Maysak flooding kills 39 in southern China after a dam breach
story.summary: A breached reservoir in Nanning drove the Guangxi toll to 39; ~130,000 evacuated amid record rainfall.
story.display_body: The toll in Guangxi jumped from six to 39, most in Nanning, where a breached reservoir sent torrents through the city (26 dead there alone), the vice mayor said Thursday. About 130,000 people have been evacuated and more than 8,000 rescuers with ~5,700 boats deployed after record rainfall — over 90 cm in the worst-hit areas. Waters are receding but more rain is forecast.

story.headline: Federal audit office rebukes Bern over troubled key IT projects
story.summary: The EFK synthesis report on 24 audits flags problems in federal digitalisation, including the botched unemployment-benefit payout system.
story.display_body: The Swiss Federal Audit Office (EFK) published a synthesis report Wednesday drawing on 24 of its 2024-25 audits of high-risk federal digitalisation projects, singling out the modernisation of unemployment-benefit payouts (which hit serious problems on rollout earlier this year) and the army's new digitalisation platform. The Federal Chancellery accepted that some projects face 'complex stakeholders and a difficult environment,' but countered that ~200 projects a year finish, 'the great majority successfully,' and that in March the Federal Council tasked it with tightening oversight of the state's digital transformation.
story.why: It is a pointed public-accountability flag on how the Confederation spends and governs its biggest IT budgets.

story.headline: Swiss doctors revolt over a new daily billing cap
story.summary: A federal cap on billable tax points per workday from 2027 sparks a petition against FMH president Yvonne Gilli; GPs largely exempt.
story.display_body: From next year, a federal rule (part of the 'cost-containment package 2' that Parliament passed in March 2025) will cap the number of billable 'tax points' (the tariff units behind each medical act) a doctor can charge per working day. Specialists with their own practices say the cap punishes speed and experience — some claim they would effectively have to stop billing by midday — and a petition now demands the resignation of FMH (the Swiss medical association) president Yvonne Gilli, who says the FMH always opposed hard caps and has softened the rule so the limit only has to hold on a monthly average. GPs and paediatricians say they are barely affected.
story.why: It is the latest front in Switzerland's fight to hold down health-insurance premiums, and it pits cost control directly against how much clinical efficiency is allowed to pay.

story.headline: A data-centre build turns canton Schaffhausen into a battleground over power use
story.summary: A US firm's data centre in Beringen will nearly double the canton's electricity use, prompting protests and cantonal legislation.
story.display_body: A data centre that US firm Stack Infrastructure is building in Beringen (population ~5,000) will, when operational around 2028, take roughly 75% of the municipality's — and nearly double the whole canton's — electricity consumption, delivering 36 megawatts of computing capacity across ~18,000 m2 and using an adiabatic cooling system equal to the annual water use of 344 households. The cantonal parliament is trying to legislate, the commune is playing it down, and activists are protesting on site.
story.why: It is a concrete Swiss instance of the AI-era data-centre boom colliding with local energy and water limits — a fight likely to repeat across the country.

story.headline: June 2026 was Western Europe's hottest on record
story.summary: Copernicus: Western Europe averaged 20.74C in June, 3.05C above normal and the second-warmest June globally, driving heat and wildfire risk.
story.display_body: The EU's Copernicus Climate Change Service reported Western Europe averaged 20.74 C in June — 3.05 C above the 1991-2020 norm and past the June-2025 record — during the second-warmest June globally, driven by record ocean surface temperatures. A late-June heatwave broke daily records across the continent, feeding wildfires in Iberia and southern France; in Switzerland, SRF is tracking heat and active fire-risk warnings and burning bans.
story.why: Back-to-back record Junes underline that Europe's summers are warming faster than the global average, with direct drought, fire and health consequences at home.
